coredata.dc:title: Characterization of novel anthocyanins film @ carbon quantum dot nanofiber intelligent active double-layer film, physicochemical properties and fresh-keeping monitoring in Ictalurus punctatus fish

coredata.dc:description: Anthocyanins and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were produced from highland barley bran to lower the waste of the cereal. This study successfully prepared a polyvinyl alcohol/soy protein isolate/anthocyanin@ polyvinyl alcohol/chitosan quaternary ammonium salt/CQDs nanofiber smart double-layer film (PSA@PHC). We explored the functions of anthocyanin film (PSA) and CQDs nanofiber film (PHC) as the inner and outer layers through structural characterization and property determination. The results showed that as the CQDs in nanofiber film rose, the diameter of the nanofibers expanded by 42.82 ± 9.37 nm, the free radical scavenging ability of DPPH and ABTS was 77.95 ± 2.53 % and 93.15 ± 0.70 %, respectively. When exposed to a xenon lamp, the bilayer film’s CQDs generated reactive oxygen species, enhancing the inhibition of S. aureus and E. coli by 67.70 ± 0.54 % and 29.90 ± 1.87 %, respectively. The potential applications for PSA@PHC smart active double-layer film in food packaging were highly promising due to its outstanding properties. In terms of its application to the preservation and freshness monitoring of Ictalurus punctatus fish, the double layer film could extend the shelf life for 2 d and enable effective monitoring.

head.ce:title: Characterization of novel anthocyanins film @ carbon quantum dot nanofiber intelligent active double-layer film, physicochemical properties and fresh-keeping monitoring in Ictalurus punctatus fish

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this figure, anthocyanins and CQDs powders prepared from barley bran were applied to the film matrix. The anthocyanin film was used as the outer color developing film, and then the CQDs electrospinning blend was spun onto the outer film to obtain a bilayer film. Subsequently, the structure and properties of the bilayer film were measured.

Anthocyanins and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were produced from highland barley bran to lower the waste of the cereal. This study successfully prepared a polyvinyl alcohol/soy protein isolate/anthocyanin@ polyvinyl alcohol/chitosan quaternary ammonium salt/CQDs nanofiber smart double-layer film (PSA@PHC). We explored the functions of anthocyanin film (PSA) and CQDs nanofiber film (PHC) as the inner and outer layers through structural characterization and property determination. The results showed that as the CQDs in nanofiber film rose, the diameter of the nanofibers expanded by 42.82 ± 9.37 nm, the free radical scavenging ability of DPPH and ABTS was 77.95 ± 2.53 % and 93.15 ± 0.70 %, respectively. When exposed to a xenon lamp, the bilayer film’s CQDs generated reactive oxygen species, enhancing the inhibition of S. aureus and E. coli by 67.70 ± 0.54 % and 29.90 ± 1.87 %, respectively. The potential applications for PSA@PHC smart active double-layer film in food packaging were highly promising due to its outstanding properties. In terms of its application to the preservation and freshness monitoring of Ictalurus punctatus fish, the double layer film could extend the shelf life for 2 d and enable effective monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, with the increasing concern of consumers about food safety, conventional PE plastic wrap used for food preservation packaging had been unable to meet market demand. To meet the diversity need of food packaging and extend food storage period [1,2], researchers focused on green packaging by applying advanced technologies and materials. With observable changes in color, a smart colorimetric film was able to deliver information to consumers about the quality and freshness of packaged foods [3].

Anthocyanins, recognized as a natural and secure active material, are applicable in producing intelligent film and packaging food for freshness monitoring [4]. Highland barley bran, a by-product of highland barley flour processing, is often employed as animal feed or discarded as waste [5]. However, it contains abundant anthocyanins that can be extracted and utilized [6]. In addition to anthocyanins, other polymers are required to synthesize smart film. Soy protein isolate (SPI) is also a by-product of processing, with low cost and excellent film-forming properties [7]. Films prepared by SPI often exhibit poor mechanical strength, high water sensitivity and inadequate barrier properties, thus limiting their commercial application [8]. Nevertheless, the oxygen barrier and mechanical properties of Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) are excellent, and when combined with SPI through hydrogen bonding, PVA/SPI composite films with superior performance can be prepared [9,10]. Combining PVA/SPI film with anthocyanins does not significantly alter the color of anthocyanins.

Apart from its excellent color rendering and mechanical properties, smart anthocyanin packaging film possesses antibacterial and antioxidant properties, which prolong the storage time of food. Since 2014, there has been a surge in interest in carbon nanomaterials for antimicrobial applications in the scientific community [11]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as popular choice for film production due to their photodynamic antimicrobial, antioxidant, low toxicity, and water solubility. [12]. In a recent study, the use of turmeric CQDs in a recent study significantly improved the antimicrobial characteristics of chitosan nanocomposite film when exposed to light, effectively hindering microbial growth [13]. Similarly, ZnO/CQDs composite coating enhanced the antibacterial performance of the stent under natural light [14]. The research indicated that CQDs could serve as a photosensitizer, generating reactive oxygen species to eradicate bacteria upon light exposure. This special material is directly added to the polymer matrix, which will affect the color of the polymer film, thus impacting the color rendering function of the anthocyanin film. Consequently, this research employed electrospinning technology and CQDs to load anthocyanins film to create a bilayer smart active film. Instead of altering the film’s color, this technology enhanced its antibacterial and antioxidant attributes. Electrospinning technology is an effective way to encapsulate the active substance, minimize environmental impact, and ensure controlled release [15]. PVA, a synthetic polymer that is both biocompatible and biodegradable, dissolves in water and is low in toxicity, can be electrospun into high-quality fibers [16]. Nevertheless, achieving stability in spinning jets requires a specific conductivity to overcome surface tension, and incorporating a specific salt or polyelectrolyte could enhance the electrical conductivity of the polymer matrix solution. Chitosan quaternary ammonium salt (HACC) is a positively charged polymer whose hydroxyl and amine groups form hydrogen bonds with the hydroxyl group of PVA, thereby regulating the repulsion between positive and negative charges and increasing chain entanglement [17]. The incorporation of HACC facilitates the dispersion of CQDs and ensures a more uniform distribution of fibers. In recent years, despite extensive research conducted on double-layer films, yet few attempts have been made to enhance their characteristics by incorporating anthocyanins and CQDs as active components in both internal and external double-layer.

We hypothesized that producing anthocyanins and CQDs from highland barley bran would prove advantageous for the utilization of diverse waste materials, considering the interconnected attributes of the complex. Anthocyanins had been found to be compatible with soy protein isolates and polyethylene glycol blends through hydrogen bonds, which increased the stability of the structure. The anthocyanins smart film, as the outer layer of the bilayer film, could monitor the food freshness. Secondly, the addition of CQDs can improve the antioxidant and antimicrobial properties of the film without affecting freshness monitoring. Electrostatic spinning technology was employed to enclose the highly active CQDs in the fiber film, thus enhancing the antioxidant and bacteriostatic characteristics of the film. Under light exposure, CQDs released more reactive oxygen species (ROS) molecules, thus inhibiting bacterial growth. HACC, being a cationic polymer, not only increased the electrical conductivity of electrostatic spinning blends, but also promoted even fibers distribution and acted as a co-inhibitor with CQDs.

In this study, CQDs nanofiber films were combined with anthocyanin films to prepare a double-layer intelligent coloring film. This film not only exhibited better mechanical properties than single-layer films, but also enables real-time observation of food freshness, tracking of color changes in food packaging, and extension of food shelf life.

Highland barley bran was purchased from Jurong Guqingfang Agricultural Products Co., LTD. (Jiangsu, China), while polyvinyl alcohol (polymerization 1799 ± 50), anhydrous citric acid and glycerol were purchased from Chengdu Colon Chemical Co., LTD., Chitosan quaternary ammonium salt (degree of substitution 98 %) was sourced from Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD., and soy protein isolate was purchased from Source Leaf Biology, E.coli (CMCC44102) and S.aureus (CMCC26003) were purchased from Beijing Biological Preservation Center, DPPH (97 %) and ABTS (98 %) were obtained from Beijing Solaibao Technology Co., LTD. LB broth and LB nutrient agar were purchased from Qingdao Gaoke Yuanhaibo Biotechnology Co., LTD.

First, 3.0 g highland barley bran was dispersed in 70 mL ultra-pure water and uniformly distributed using a vortex oscillator. The solution was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave chamber and heated at 200 °C for 10 h [18]. Following the reaction, the solution was centrifuged to separate the precipitates and insoluble particles and cooled to room temperature. The supernatant was obtained, and then it was filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane and subjected to dialysis using ultra-pure water for 48 h, with water changes every 8 h. The resulting liquid in the dialysis bag was freeze-dried to obtain the CQDs solid powder sample.

The morphology of CQDs was observed by transmission electron microscope (FEI). Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (NICOLET IS 10, Thermo Field Instruments, USA) was utilized to determine the functional group structure of CQDs in the range of 4000–500 cm−1. UV spectrum determination of CQDs was measured using a UV-1800PC visible spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra of CQDs were recorded by Lumina fluorescence spectrophotometer.

Hela cells were seeded in 96-well plates are a density of 5 × 103 cells per well and cultivated overnight in a 37 °C, 5 % CO2 incubator, until reaching in the logarithmic growth phase. Various concentrations of CQDs (0–500 g/mL) were added to the wells, followed by co-incubation with 100 µL of sterile PBS. After 24 h of incubation at 37 °C, the supernatant was removed, and 10 µL of 10 % CCK-8 media was added to each well. Following an additional 1 h of incubation at 5 % CO2, 37 °C in a constant temperature incubator, the absorbance at 450 nm was measured using a microplate reader to assess the cell viability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS production by CQDs in Dark/Light conditions
ce:section.ce:para: A 100 µM solution of 2′-7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) was prepared in dimethyl sulfoxide. To achieve the final concentration of 10 µM 2′-7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCFH) 10 mL of PBS buffer (0.1 mM, pH=7.4) was added to stop hydrolysis after mixing evenly 0.5 mL of DCFH-DA solution with 0.01 M sodium hydroxide for 30 min in the dark. The DCFH solution was chilled in foil before use. A mixture of 100 mL of DCFH solution and 2 mL of CQDs solution (500 µg/mL) was incubated for 1 h under xenon lamp illumination. Under four sets of conditions, ROS production was determined by evaluating the intensity of the fluorescence from the oxidation of non-fluorescent DCFH to fluorescent DCF after incubation [19].

CQDs were centrifuged (4000 r/min) for 5 min to obtain the precipitate, and washed twice with PBS. DCFH-DA was diluted with PBS at 1:1000 to a final concentration of 10 µmol/L. The sample was then suspended in the diluted DCFH-DA, and the light group remained irradiated for 5 h, while the dark group was not irradiated. The mixture was inverted every 3–5 min to ensure full contact between the probe and the sample. A 500 µL of the above treated CQDs solution was added to a confocal dish for direct observation using a multi-photon laser confocal microscope (Leica SP8 DIVE). For parameter settings: Activate the argon ion laser, select the 488 nm excitation channel, set the emission wavelength in the range of 500–550 nm, and capture images with dual-channel excitation at 410 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of anthocyanins from highland barley bran
ce:section.ce:para: The highland barley bran ethanol solution (1:10, w/v) was prepared and 1 % citric acid was added to enhance the stability of anthocyanins. After thorough mixing, the solution was stirred in a water bath at 50 °C for 2 h. Following extraction, the supernatant was cooled, centrifuged, and evaporated to 1/5 of the original volume at 50 °C. The concentrated fluids were then freeze-dried to obtain anthocyanins powder samples.

A 3 % (w/v) solution of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and soy protein isolate (SPI) was prepared. 1.5 % anthocyanins powder was added to the combined solutions in a 1:1 vol ratio. Subsequently, ultrasonic and vacuum methods were employed to eliminate bubbles from the film solution after thorough stirring. The resulting 80 g film liquid was uniformly cast on a plastic plate (23 × 23 cm) before being dried in a 40 °C oven. After 12–13 h, the film, named PSA film, was unwrapped and balanced in the incubator for 48 h (25℃, 50 %) [20].

To obtain the electrospinning blend, the HACC, CQDs and PVA were dissolved in distilled water successively. CQDs powder (0, 0. 009, 0.01, 0.02, 0.03 g) was then added to the solution, which was stirred in a magnetic stirring water bath at 95℃ for 3 h. The electrospinning blend was ultrasonically dispersed by an ultrasonic cleaner for 30 min and vacuumed to remove the bubbles. Subsequently, the blend solution was loaded into a 10 mL syringe, with an applied voltage of 20 kV and a feed rate of 0.5 mL/h. The collection distance was set at 8 cm, with the collection drum covered by the prepared PSA film. Under the action of an elevated electrostatic force, the spinning liquid was sprayed in an electric field to form fibers deposited on the PSA film, forming a bilayer film. The PH film in the case of a single-layer fiber film indicated the absence of CQDs in the blend, whereas the PHC film suggested the inclusion of CQDs in the blend. The bilayer film containing anthocyanins, with varying amounts of CQDs added, were designated as PSA@PHC00, PSA@PHC09, PSA@PHC10, PSA@PHC20, and PSA@PHC30, respectively.

The structure of the film was analyzed using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. The tests were performed on ATR crystals with air as a blank background and a scanning range of 650–4000 cm −1. Scanning electron microscopy (Thermo Feld Quattro S) was employed to observe the front and back micromorphologies of the film at 5000 and 20,000 magnification. Cross-sectional morphology of the sample was examined under liquid nitrogen embrittlement fracture at 2000-fold amplification. X-ray diffractometer (Japanese Science-smartlab) was utilized to analyze the XRD pattern of the film sample at 5-80°. The thermal stability of the film at 40-600℃ was assessed by a synchronous thermal analyzer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo Field Nexsa) was used to test the XPS spectrum of the film sample with an operating voltage of 15 kV and an operating current of 10 mA. Then the sample was analyzed under vacuum (5 × 10−10 Pa) and passed through a monochromatic Al Kα light source with an energy of 50 eV (inspection scan). All peaks were corrected with the binding energy of 284.8 eV variable carbon C1s peaks.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the performance of PSA@PHC film
Thickness, water Content, dissolution Rate, and swelling degree of PSA@PHC film
ce:section.ce:para:
The thickness of the film was measured at 10 random positions by a digital thickness meter, and the average value was calculated. Measurements were recorded in millimeters (mm) to four significant digits. A 2 × 2 cm film was weighed into a weighing bottle of 30 × 50 mm and dried to a constant weight in a hot air oven at 105℃. The initial mass of the film was recorded as W0 before drying, and the mass after drying to a constant weight was recorded as W1. The moisture content of the film was then calculated according to the formula (2). Subsequently, the film sample was immersed in 20 mL of distilled water and left at room temperature for 24 h. After removing the water from the beaker, the film sample was dried at 105℃ to a constant weight and weighed as W2. The dissolution rate of the film was calculated using the formula (3). Another film sample was taken to determine the initial mass W0, then placed in a weighing bottle and left to stand for 24 h. The mass of the swollen film sample was measured as W3, and the swelling degree was by the formula (4). Each film sample underwent triplicate testing, and an average value for moisture content, dissolution rate, and swelling degree were calculated accordingly:

The tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EAB) of film were tested using the HD-B609B-S universal test machine. Film samples with smooth surface and uniform thickness were selected. The initial clip distance was set to 20 mm, the moving speed of the clamp was set to 30 mm/min. The measurements were taken until the film ruptured, and the data were recorded. Nine parallels were used for each film, and their mean values were calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ammonia sensitive response characteristics of PSA@PHC film
ce:section.ce:para: A 20 × 20 mm film was placed above a centrifuge tube filled with 5 % ammonia solution, and the color change of the film was recorded with a color difference meter every 10 min until the color of the film no longer changed. Then, in order to assess the reversibility of the film, the reacted film was put at the mouth of a centrifugal tube filled with a solution of 10 % acetic acid, and the color shift of the film was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of antioxidant activity of PSA@PHC film
ce:section.ce:para:
According to Lin et al [21], the antioxidant capacity of film was determined by modification. The ABTS radical scavenging involved the addition of 0.1 g of the film to 5 mL of 50 % ethanol solution, followed by a 12 h oscillation, centrifugation, and set aside of the sample solution. Subsequently, a 7 mmol/L ABTS solution was mixed with a 2.6 mmol/L potassium perovskite solution in equal proportions, and reacted in the dark room temperature for 12 h. The ABTS solution was then diluted with methanol until the absorption value was within 0.70 ± 0.02. The absorbance of the solution was determined at 734 nm by mixing 1 mL of sample solution and 2 mL of ABTS solution well and incubating it in darkness for 30 min. The ABTS radical scavenging activity was calculated using the formula (5):

For DPPH radical scavenging, 0.1 mmol/L of DPPH ethanol solution was mixed with the sample solution, incubated in darkness for 30 min, and the absorbance of the solution was measured at 517 nm. The DPPH radical scavenging activity was calculated as follows:

ce:section.ce:section-title: The barrier properties of PSA@PHC film
ce:section.ce:para:
For water vapor transmittance (WVP), 10 g of calcium chloride was placed into a 30 × 50 mm weighing bottle, with the bottle’s weight(m)recorded with a precision of 0.0001 g. A 60 × 60 mm film specimen was sliced and utilized to seal the weighing bottle, which was secured with a rubber band. The processed weighing container was situated in an incubator at 25 °C and 90 % humidity. The weight of the bottle was measured every 12 h until it stabilized, and the weight increment (ΔM) was recorded. The procedure was replicated three times, and the mean value was calculated [22]. The following formula was used to calculate The film’s WVP:
(7)
W V P =
Δ M × D
A × T × Δ P
where D is the thickness of the film sample (mm), A is the effective area of water vapor transmission (m2), T is the equilibrium time (s), and ΔP is the partial pressure difference of water vapor on both sides of the film, Pa.

For oxygen transmittance (OP), a 3 g sample of deoxidizer was placed in a 30 × 50 mm weighing container, and its weight was documented with a precision of 0.0001 g. The film specimen was trimmed to a size of 60 × 60 mm to seal the container, which was secured with a rubber band. The processed weighing container was situated in an incubator at 25 °C and 75 % humidity. The weight of the bottle was measured every 12 h until its weight stabilized, and the incremental weight gain (ΔM) was recorded. This process was repeated 3 times to obtain an average value [23]. The formula below was employed to calculate the OP for films:
(8)
O P =
Δ M × D
A × T × Δ P
where D is the thickness of the film sample (mm), A is the effective area of water vapor transmission (m2), T is the equilibrium time (s), and ΔP is the partial pressure difference of water vapor on both sides of the film, Pa.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The antibacterial properties of PSA@PHC film
ce:section.ce:para:
Using the method described by Chen et al [18], E. coli and S. aureus in the glycerol tube were inoculated into LB broth and cultured at 37℃ for 12 h. The bacterial suspension was adjusted to OD600 of 0.5 was measured, resulting in a concentration of about 1 × 108 CFU/mL. A 0.1 g film was UV-sterilized for 1 h, then a 3 mL bacterial solution was added to the tube containing the film. The samples were irradiated with a xenon lamp for 5 h, with simultaneous dark and blank controls. After incubation, the bacterial solution was diluted to a suitable concentration, and 100 µL was inoculated on LB agar plates and cultured in an incubator at 37℃ for 24 h. Colony counts were performed using the ImageJ software.
(9)

where, N0 represents the number of bacteria in the dark group; N1 represents the number of bacteria in the light group.

Dead and live bacteria of E.coli and S.aureus were photographed by PSA@PHC30 film using confocal laser microscopy (Leica SP8 DIVE). The dead bacterial solution was obtained by treating the activated bacterial solution with 0.5 % formaldehyde solution for 24 h. The film was cut into 1 cm discs and placed in ultra-clean table under ultraviolet irradiation for 120 min. The live bacterial solution and the dead bacterial solution (1 × 108 CFU/mL) were incubated with the membrane samples respectively for 5 h under light shaking conditions. Prior to fluorescence imaging, all experimental samples were centrifugally washed 6 times with sterile PBS at 4000 r/min to remove media and CQDs that did not enter bacterial cells. The laser confocal microscope parameter Settings are consistent with those in 2.2.3.

After washing and drying, the fish was sliced and cooled in the fridge. Following the pre-cooling phase, the fish weighing 60 g were relocated to seperate crisper containers, forming three groups: the control group, the PSA group, and the PSA@PHC30 group. In the control group, no treatment was applied. In the experiment, PSA film and PSA@PHC30 film (6 × 6 cm) were placed at the crisper box’s bottom, whereas PSA film and PSA@PHC30 film (4 × 4 cm) were placed at its top. After packaging, the fish samples were stored at 4 °C for 8 d with consistent LED light exposure. Fish freshness was assessed every 2 d by measuring film color, volatile base nitrogen (TVB-N), aerobic plate count (TVC), and related electronic nose markers.

A color difference meter was employed to measure film color. The TVB-N was determined using the national standard GB5009.228-2016; the TVC was determined according to the standard GB4789.2-2022.The gas composition was analyzed by electronic nose, with a cleaning time of 60 s, a test time of 60 s, and a constant flow rate.

SPSS software was used for one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA), and the results were statistically analyzed, with p < 0.05 indicating statistical significance. All experiments were repeated at least three times using Origin for mapping analysis. All values represented standard deviation ± standard deviation.

Fig. 1 A from the TEM demonstrated that CQDs were spherical particles with even distribution. 150 particles were measured for particle size distribution using Image J software, resulting in an average particle diameter of approximately 2.88 nm CQDs, as indicated by the fitting curve. The lattice spacing of CQDs was observed to be 0.2035 nm in Fig. 1B. The FTIR (Fig. 1C) revealed that the 3395 cm−1 absorption peak was the elongated vibrational peak of O-H. The stretching vibrations of CO, C–N, C–C, and C–O were identified as the cause of the absorption peaks at 1689 cm−1, 1386 cm−1, 1211 cm−1, and 1082 cm−1, respectively. The analysis revealed that the CQDs had a high concentration of oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g., carboxyl group, hydroxyl group) on their surface, indicating superior water solubility. In UV–vis spectrogram (Fig. 1D), there was a significant UV absorption peak at 277 nm, which might be attributed to the π → π* transition of the CC group [24]. The aqueous solution of CQDs exhibited a yellowish hue, and when illuminated with UV365 nm lamps, it emitted blue light, as corroborated by the emission spectrum in Fig. 1E. Fig. 1F showed the emission spectra of CQDs solution at different excitation wavelengths (330–490 nm). It could be seen that the emission wavelength of CQDs significantly redshifted with increasing excitation wavelength, indicating a clear excitation dependence of the luminescence of CQDs solutions. This could be attributed to the defects on the CQDs’ surface and the quantity and positioning of the emission points [25]. The addition of CQDs to the film solution, in conjunction with the structural benefits of CQDs, could facilitate CQDs to form hydrogen bonds with other compounds and spread evenly in the film solution.

The impact of concentrations of CQDs (0 to 500 μg/mL) on Hela cells was examined. As shown in Fig. 2 A, when the concentration of CQDs was increased to 500 μg/mL, the cell survival rate was 94 %, and the IC50:1007 μg/mL was determined based on the function. These results demonstrated the biocompatibility of the synthesized CQDs, which were derived from organic raw highland barley bran. These findings were consistent with cytotoxic results obtained for chia seed CQDs synthesized by Marouzi et al. [26]. The utilization of CQDs in food packaging was deemed safe given their minimal toxicity within a specific range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS produced by CQDs in Dark/Light conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) were highly reactive oxygen atoms, including hydroxyl radicals (OH·), singlet oxygen (1O2), superoxide anions (O2 –), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) [27]. ROS possessed the capability to eliminate specific bacteria. Fig. 2B clearly showed that CQDs had the ability to produce ROS in both dark and light conditions, with heightened production of ROS when exposed to light. ROS could be generated under light, as well as from other biological processes. When CQDs were exposed to light, the transformation of oxygen into 1O2 via electron transfer caused the demise of bacteria due to their photochemical characteristics [28]. CQDs exhibited photodynamic bacteriostasis, thereby significantly extending the duration of food storage when utilized in food packaging. Using confocal microscopy, none of the groups without CQDs produced any fluorescence. Either the fluorescence produced was weak or could not be observed, while the group with CQDs produced more fluorescence with CQDs. When illuminated with CQDs, there was a noted increase in fluorescence, which corresponded well to the fluorescence spectrum of ROS.

The SEM of the front, back, and cross-section of the film, as well as the distribution of fiber diameters, were shown in Fig. 3 . In terms of the film’s overall section and surface, the incorporation of CQDs did not result in drastic alterations to the area and exterior of the film. The PSA film had a flat surface, which could have been due to uneven mixing near the hot air outlet during drying, resulting in protrusions and cracks. As the number of CQDs rose, the PHC nanofiber film’s surface diameter tended to expand. An increase in CQDs led to a heightened viscosity in the mixture used for electrospinning, which resisted axial stretching during electrospinning, thus producing coarser fibers [29]. The absence of beading on the fibers suggested that the CQDs were evenly distributed throughout the polymer matrix [30]. The fiber film exhibited specific pores when observed from the cross-section, and the anthocyanins film exhibited a layered arrangement, allowing for seamless integration on its surface.

The structures and functional groups of single-layer PSA and PH film and bilayer PSA@PHC film were analyzed by FTIR. As shown in Fig. 4 (A-B), the absorption bands at 3299 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of O-H/N-H bonds, and the absorption bands at 2990 cm−1 and 2941 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of C–H bonds [31]. The absorption band at 1729 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of the anthocyanins characteristic peak CC [32]. Additionally, the absorption bands at 1423 cm−1, 1087 cm−1, and 840 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of C–H, N–H, and O–H bonds [33]. The film formed by all the different components in the figure had similar functional group structures, indicating that the interaction of different complexes would not affect the structure of the overall complex [34]. The functional groups were rich in a single component, and the number of functional groups was reduced after preparation of the complex, showing a common functional group of a single component. When CQDs were added to the film, the peak strength changed, which might be due to the non-covalent and hydrogen bonding between CQDs and other components [13]. However, the peak position did not shift, indicating that CQDs had good compatibility in the blend. With the increase of CQDs, the functional groups tended to be stable and the peak value changed little, indicating successful encapsulation in the fiber film.

XRD patterns (Fig. 4C-D) showed that CQDs have limited crystallinity, with distinct diffraction peaks at 19–23° for each component. Both single-layer PSA and PH film and bilayer PSA@PHC film had diffraction peaks at 19.57°, indicating that CQDs was uniformly dispersed in the matrix of PVA and HACC electrospinning blends. With the increase of CQDs, the peak intensity first increased and then decreased, becoming sharper. The increase in CQDs led to hydrogen bond rearrangement with the film matrix, which made the structure of each component of the film more closely connected. However, when CQDs increased beyond a certain amount, the mutual movement of molecular chains in the film matrix was restricted, decreasing the crystallinity [35]. The peak XRD intensity variation was similar to that observed in the nanoparticle film by Luo et al [36]. Furthermore, there were no other obvious characteristic peaks in the spectra of PSA@PHC nanofiber film, suggesting that electrospinning could prevent film crystallization and promote the formation of amorphous structures [37].

The TG image (Fig. 4E-F) displayed three significant weight loss stages for the film. In the first stage (40–150 °C), the weight loss was primarily due to the evaporation of water molecules in the film. The PH film showed practically no weight loss due to its low moisture content, which was consistent with the test results of water content. The second stage (150–320 °C) involved the degradation of C–C main chain of PVA and the decomposition of SPI chain [38,39]. The PH film experienced weight loss at 200–320 °C due to the loss of water, followed by a sharp decrease in weight after 320 °C attributed to the degradation of PVA main chain. In the third stage, after 400 °C, complete carbonization and decomposition of PVA and SPI molecules occurred at elevated temperatures. with the PH film exhibiting higher weight loss due to the presence of the only a macromolecular PVA. The addition of CQDs had a negligible effect on the thermal stability of the bilayer, but the PH film loaded onto the PSA film reduced the thermal loss rate by up to 8 %. Therefore, the PSA@PHC bilayer film could enhance the overall stability of the film, which was consistent with the results reported in the literature [40].

XPS was used to analyze the chemical composition of composite film surfaces. Fig. 4G displayed the XPS spectra of the PSA@PHC00 and PSA@PHC30 composite films, with the two peaks corresponding to C1s (285.08 eV) and O1s (532.08 eV), respectively, and with no shift in the position of the two peaks in both films. Fig. 4 H-K showed that the O1s spectra and C1s spectra corresponded to the PSA@PHC00 and PSA@PHC30 composite films, respectively. The binding energies at 284.58 eV, 286.60 eV, and 287.51 eV in the C1s spectrum of PSA@PHC00 films corresponded to the characteristic bonds of C-C/C-H, C-O, and CO, respectively [41]. After the incoporation of CQDs to PSA@PHC30 film, the C1s peaks were 284.57 eV, 286.05 eV, and 287.54 eV, respectively, with changes in the binding energy of more than 0.2 eV, indicating that CQDs interacted well in the film [42]. 531.98 eV and 532.6 eV in the O1s spectrum in the PSA@PHC00 film corresponded to CO and C–O–H characteristic bonds, respectively. Upon the addition of CQDs to PSA@PHC30 films, the O1s peaks were located at 531.91 eV and 532.46 eV, respectively, The bond binding energies corresponding to the O1s spectra were reduced, with the decrease of 0.07 eV and 0.14 eV, respectively. According to the XPS results, the bilayer film contained many oxygen-containing functional groups and exhibited good water solubility, which was consistent with the results of FTIR. This further indicated that the composite film components could mix well and interact favorably.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of properties of PSA@PHC film
Film thickness, moisture content, water solubility, swelling rate
ce:section.ce:para: In Table 1 , the PSA@PHC film exhibited uniform thickness, indicating that the addition of CQDs would not affect the film’s thickness. The PH film had a low moisture content due to solvent evaporation during electrospinning [43]. There was slight difference between double PSA@PHC film and PSA film, because the PH film was tightly bonded to the PSA film, preventing significant dissolution. Regarding the film’s swelling degree, the PH film showed extreme swelling, indicating strong water absorption. Both PVA and HACC in the liquid matrix of the PH film were hydrophilic substances. The water absorption of PSA@PHC film was slightly enhanced due to the increase of CQDs, which contained a large number of hydrophilic carboxyl and hydroxyl groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color Properties, ammonia sensitive color rendering properties of PSA@PHC film
ce:section.ce:para: Color of film was shown in Table 2 , the incorporation of the white PH film onto the PSA film affected the brightness (L value) of the PSA film, reducing it by 12.68. With the increase of CQDs, the yellowness (b value) increased while the redness (a value) decreased. Ammonia-sensitive reactivity and the discoloration of the film at different times were shown in Fig. 5 . As the ammonia reaction time increased from 0 to 180 min, the colors of PSA film and PSA@PHC film shifted from red to light red, light green, green and dark green, while the PH film still maintained white. The ΔE value of the PSA@PHC film rose to 20–25, whereas the ΔE value of PSA film rose to 5 after 60 min reaction The ΔE value of the PH film remained unchanged, indicating that the bilayer film had better sensitivity with ΔE value >5, which was easily observable by human eyes [44]. After combining PSA and PHC film, the PHC film, as the inner layer, had hydrophilicity and fiber porosity [45], allowing ammonia molecules to penetrate the inner layer more easily and reach the outer PSA film, thereby enhancing color sensitive. Additionally, placing the ammonia-reacted film in 10 % acetic acid solution for 60 min changed the film color from dark green to light red, demonstrating the reversibility of the color change. Elevating the concentration of CQDs in film slightly affected its sensitivity. Overall, the PSA@PHC bilayer film exhibited more sensitivity to ammonia, ensuring prompt and accurate indication of food freshness in packaging to cater to consumers preferences.

By incorporating PVA, the study improved the mechanical attributes of the SPI film. It could be seen from the Fig. 6 A that the tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EAB) of PSA film were 6.32 ± 0.34 MPa and 124.30 ± 6.80 %, respectively, which were lower than the TS and EAB values of PVA/SPI film measured by Yan et al [46]. It showed that anthocyanins cross-linked different components in SPI and PVA substrates through hydrogen bonding and electrostatic attraction, creating stress concentration points between molecules and slightly weakening the mechanical properties of the formed PSA film [47]. The PH film, layered through the fiber, was prone to breakage, resulting in low ductility and requiring higher tensile strength than PSA film. The TS and EAB of monolayer film could be enhanced by loading PHC film onto PSA film. The addition of CQDs, which was tiny and contained hydrophilic groups, dispersed uniformly in the polymer matrix through hydrogen bonding [48], thereby enhancing the tensile strength and flexibility of PSA@PHC film. The FTIR results confirmed the formation of hydrogen bonds, contributing to the enhanced mechanical properties of the double-layer film [49]. These findings aligned with the results of Ezati et al [50], where low concentrations of CQDs could enhance the mechanical properties of the film. The incorporation of high tensile strength and flexibility could enhance its applicability in food package, making the PSA@PHC double-layer film an effective solution to the vulnerability of CQDs nanofiber film to rupture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidation resistance of the PSA@PHC film
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in the Fig. 6B, both PSA and PH film demonstrated a certain level of antioxidant capacity, due to the presence of two antioxidant substances (anthocyanins and HACC) within them. Its oxidation resistance was further enhanced by the composite nature of the film. With the gradual increase of CQDs, the free radical scavenging rates of ABTS and DPPH increased by 6.94 ± 0.28 % and 22.81 ± 0.44 %, respectively. CQDs exhibited potent antioxidant characteristics owing to the substantial presence of oxygen groups in the joint pathways, facilitating both hydrogen and electron transport. Furthermore, the H metastasis in CQDs hydroxyl diminished ABTS and DPPH free radicals, leading to enhanced stability of ABTS-H and DPPH-H [51]. The excellent antioxidant activity of PSA@PHC30 film could be attributed to its surface functional groups involved in free radical scavenging [52]. Because the dissolution rate of PSA@PHC30 film in ABTS aqueous solution was higher than that of ethanol/DPPH solution, the film with added CQDs had better scavenging effects on ABTS free radical. The PSA@PHC30 film exhibited unparalleled antioxidant properties, prolonged food shelf life, and effectively prevented oxidation in food packaging.

The water vapor and oxygen barrier properties of the film were key factors in food quality. As seen from the values of WVP and OP in Fig. 6, the bilayer composition had a more significant effect on oxygen barrier properties. The PH film demonstrated the best water vapor barrier performance. In an environment with 90 % humidity, the PSA film contained a large amount of anthocyanins polar groups, making it easier for polar water molecules to pass through. However, when the surface of the PSA film was occupied by a large number of water molecules, the PH film’s strong water absorption transferred additional water vapor to the interior, reducing the water vapor barrier of the PSA@PHC film. The surface of CQDs also contained numerous hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. When dispersed into the polymer matrix, the CQDs and the polymer matrix rearranged and formed fresh hydrogen bonds under the influence of temperature. Meanwhile, the CQDs were uniformly scattered in the fiber as the spinning process proceeded. Because of its hydrophilic nature, the barrier properties of the bilayer PSA@PHC film were reduced. Similarly, in Fig. 6D, at 75 % humidity, the PSA film bound to water molecules, breaking the tightness of the internal structure and making it easier for oxygen molecules to penetrate. Combining PSA with PH increased the porosity of the film, thereby weakening its oxygen barrier properties [53]. The differences between the WVP and OP of the film might be attributed to the environmental humidity, with smaller ambient humidity resulting in smaller OP values. Ambient humidity might be less than 90 % during the storage of packaged foods, and the PSA @ PHC film showed relatively good barrier properties. For breathable fruits and vegetables, the lower barrier enhanced the air flow between the environment and the internal atmosphere, facilitating the escape of internal vapor without expediting its decomposition.

The antibacterial ability of the film determined the shelf life of the food. The Fig. 7 showed that the PSA@PHC film was less effective against S. aureus and E. coli in dark conditions. However, its inhibition ability on S. aureus and E. coli was significantly enhanced under light conditions. This could be attributed to the fact that CQDs in PSA@PHC film could produce ROS when stimulated by light, which could penetrate the cell film of bacteria, destroy their proteins and DNA, and eventually lead to the bacterial death [54]. However, the inhibition ability differed between the two types of bacteria showed differences, with Gram-positive bacteria being more sensitive to CQDs than Gram-negative bacteria, due to differences in cell wall composition and CQDs binding affinity. Gram-positive bacteria, lacking the outer membrane, were more susceptible to the damaging effect of ROS, which could penetrate and degrade their cell walls [54,55]. This phenomenon was similar to the photodynamic antibacterial result observed in chitosan/sodium alginate/QDs @ ZIF-8 nanocomposite films [56]. Beyond ROS production, the antimicrobial properties of CQDs were linked to their size and distinct surface features. When bacteria infiltrated the fiber film, they bound to CQDs through electrostatic interactions, adhering to bacterial cell walls. This allowed smaller CQDs to invade the bacteria better and trigger bacterial death. CQDs were also highly soluble in water, enabling faster attachment to bacteria and leading to their demise [48,57]. The inhibitory mechanism of CQDs in PSA@PHC30 film was verified by confocal microscopy. The images showed that only dead E. coli and S. aureus could be efficiently stained by CQDs, with blue fluorescence at the excitation wavelength of 488 nm. In contrast, the live bacteria did not fluoresce under the same conditions. This selective staining was attributed to electrostatic attraction, CQDs could selectively stain dead bacteria, but the live bacteria of the two kinds of bacteria will not be stained by CQDs because of electrostatic rejection. Another reason was that when bacteria die, the cell wall could not control what got into them. Due to their small size, CQDs could penetrate damaged cell walls. The PSA@PHC30 film in the image exhibited superior antimicrobial resistance compared to the other films, while also ensuring the preservation of food freshness.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Ictalurus punctatus fish freshness monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: As ilustrated in Fig. 8 A, the control group displayed a steady transition of the fish’s hue from fresh to yellow, accompanied by an increase in its surface water content. This shift was attributed to the activities of microorganisms and internal enzymes during storage. Due to its antioxidative qualities, the breakdown of the PSA film in the fresh fish slowed down, leading to a yellow exterior and an extended shelf life. The introduction of PSA@PHC30 film extended the fish’s storage period by 2 d.

Both PSA film and PSA@PHC30 film showed color changes during the 8-day storage period of the fish, as shown in Fig. 8B. The color of the film changed from an initial dark red to light red, accurately reflecting the change in fish freshness. In Fig. 8C, ABCDE represented different storage days, with 123 corresponding to the control group, PSA group, and PSA@PHC30 group. From the PCA diagram, it could be observed that the cumulative contribution rate of the first principal component (PC1) and the second principal component (PC2) was 97.9 %, indicating that the PCA diagram could better reflect the changes of volatile odor of fish with different degrees of freshness. The smell of fish stored for 0–4 d was clustered together, indicating that the fish was fresh. The smell of the fish stored for 6–8 d in the control group was significantly different, indicating gradual deterioration [58]. Compared with the control group, the changes in the volatile odor of fish packaged with PSA film and PSA@PHC30 film were slower, demonstrating that both PSA film and PSA@PHC30 film had preservation effects, with PSA@PHC30 film showing a stronger effect than PSA film. During storage, CQDs were released slowly, and the ROS produced had a bactericidal effect. Additionally, the PSA@PHC30 fiber pad effectively slowed down the spoilage of the fish.

Volatile Basic Nitrogen (TVB-N) and aerobic plate count (TVC) served as crucial metrics for assessing food spoilage. Changes in TVB-N within fish were depicted in Fig. 8D. As storage duration extended, there was a notable rise in the TVB-N levels in the fish across the three categories. The increase in TVB-N levels was linked to the breakdown of nitrogen-rich substances in the fish by various microorganisms and enzymes [59]. Initially, the fresh fish’s TVB-N measured 6.02 mg/100 g, but by day 6, the control group surpassed the 20 mg/100 g threshold, indicating spoilage. On the sixth day, the fish in both the PSA and PSA@PHC30 film treatment groups remained within the set threshold. These findings indicated that the treatments on two types of film were effective in decelerating the protein breakdown triggered by microbes and extending the shelf life of fish.

In Fig. 8E, the total number of bacterial colonies in the fish of the three groups showed an increasing trend during the storage time 0–8d. In the early stage of storage, the growth of bacteria was relatively slow, which might be due to the small initial microbial load and an adaptation period. The total number of initial colony count under cold storage was 4.05 lgCFU/g. By the 6th day, the total number of fish colonies in the control group reached 7.86 lgCFU/g, exceeding the safe upper limit of 7 lgCFU/g for raw fish [60], suggesting spoilage. On the 6th day, the PSA film and PSA@PHC30 film inhibited the reproduction of microorganisms, thereby extending the storage time of fish.

The preservation mechanism is illustarted in Fig. 8F. Elements of the bilayer film were linked via hydrogen bonds, enhancing biocompatibility. During the fish storage, the film from the crisper released CQDs and HACC, jointly enhancing the antibacterial properties. The bacterial surface had a negative charge, and the cationic groups on HACC’s surface penetrated the bacterial cell wall, potentially altering the bacteria’s internal milieu and hindering their growth [61]. The gradual release of CQDs might be facilitated by the fiber film. Under light, CQDs produced ROS that acted on the bacterial cell membrane, inhibiting bacterial growth. CQDs could activate NADPH- oxidase in the bacterial membrane, leading to an excess of ROS in the bacteria and potentially their death [62,63]. Concurrently, the film’s anthocyanins prevented fish fat oxidation, maintaining the fish’s vibrant color. Prolonged storage resulted in reduced active ingredients, an excess of bacteria, and a steady deterioration in the fish’s quality. Consequently, hydrogen sulfide, ammonia, and other detrimental gases were emitted as the fish deteriorated. The interaction of these gases with the bilayer film altered the film’s anthocyanin composition, causing its color to shift from dark red to light red, thus facilitating the assessment of fish freshness.

Highland barley bran was utilized in the preparation of nanoscale CQDs in this study, which exhibited excellent dispersibility, hydrophilicity, and minimized toxicity. When exposed to light, CQDs were able to generate ROS and impede bacterial proliferation. Additionally, the creation of PSA@PHC bilayer smart active film was achieved. Structural characterization through FT-IR, XRD, TG showed that the bilayer smart active film had a stable structure and that the increase of CQDs slightly changed its structure. The findings indicated that the PSA@PHC bilayer film was responsive to ammonia, with its hue shifted from red to green. The film could be used in food packaging to visually assess the food freshness by its color change. The addition of CQDs to the PSA@PHC film markedly improved its mechanical, antioxidant, and antibacterial properties. Under light exposure, the ROS generated by the PSA@PHC bilayer film could be detrimental to the majority of bacteria, enabling the film preservation in illuminated settings and potentially prolonging food shelf life. Throughout an 8-d span of employing PSA film and PSA@PHC30 film for preserving fish, significant color differences were observed in the film, indicating its effectiveness in evaluating fish freshness. Moreover, the bilayer film’s ability to extend fish storage, compared to the single-layer film, enhanced the preservation efficiency of the double layer film.
